companyCaseStudy.title: How Reddit Replaced Cassandra with PostgreSQL to Cut Complexity and Tail Latency by ~10x
companyCaseStudy.summary: Reddit moved a decade-old Cassandra-backed primary data store to PostgreSQL to tame operational complexity, improve queryability and reduce tail latencies under hundreds of millions of daily requests — a migration that traded tricksy eventual-consistency guarantees for faster, simpler, more debuggable reads and writes.
companyCaseStudy.explanation:
## Hook — the problem at scale
Reddit operated a massive social platform with hundreds of millions of monthly users and traffic spiky around major events and AMAs. Their experience running Cassandra as a primary store for posts/comments and other core data introduced operational pain: complex tuning, unpredictable tail latency, expensive compactions, and tricky consistency semantics that made debugging incidents hard.

### Problem → Challenge
- Workload: millions of reads/writes per minute on hot keys (popular subreddits and posts) with large variance between steady-state and viral spikes.
- Cassandra issues: compaction storms, repair-time windows, frequent coordinator timeouts during GC/compaction, and reads that sometimes returned stale or inconsistent results requiring application-side repair logic.
- Operational cost: a squad-sized effort for every Cassandra upgrade/repair and recurring incidents during traffic spikes.

The core challenge: how to preserve throughput and availability while drastically reducing complexity, improving read consistency and cutting tail latencies.

### Solution — pragmatic migration to PostgreSQL (and targeted architecture changes)
Reddit chose to consolidate onto PostgreSQL for many workloads, keeping Cassandra only where its particular characteristics still made sense. The migration strategy was pragmatic and staged:

1. Evaluate which data fits relational semantics.
   - Timeline and post metadata (which benefit from SQL queries, strong consistency and joins) were first-class candidates.
   - Time-series telemetry and some write-heavy internal metrics remained elsewhere.

2. Build a compatibility layer (adapter / shim) to talk to both Cassandra and Postgres.
   - A lightweight service routed reads/writes while supporting dual-writes during the migration window.
   - This adapter enabled dark-launching Postgres-backed reads for verification without disrupting production traffic.

3. Move traffic incrementally by keyspace/shard.
   - Popular subreddits (hot keys) were migrated last after validating performance under heavy load.
   - Canarying: a percentage of traffic for a specific subreddit or post type was diverted to Postgres and monitored for correctness and latency.

4. Optimize Postgres for high throughput.
   - Tuned connection pooling (pgbouncer) to avoid connection explosion.
   - Horizontalization via logical sharding: each Reddit logical shard mapped to a dedicated Postgres cluster/primary; replicas handled reads.
   - Aggressive use of indexes for common access patterns (comment parent ID, post score, created_at) and batched writes to keep WAL throughput efficient.

5. Operational improvements
   - Standardized backup/restore and migrations (online schema changes using tooling like gh-ost or native pg_repack patterns).
   - Observability: added query sampling, slow-query dashboards, and per-shard tail-latency monitoring which quickly exposed rogue queries.


### Architecture (visual)
```mermaid
graph LR
  Client --> CDN[CDN / Edge]
  CDN --> API[API Fleet]
  API --> Router[Migration Router (adapter)]
  Router --> Postgres[Postgres Shard Cluster A]
  Router --> PostgresB[Postgres Shard Cluster B]
  Router --> Cassandra[Cassandra (legacy, reduced set)]
  Postgres --> ReplicaA[Read Replica(s)]
  PostgresB --> ReplicaB[Read Replica(s)]
  ReplicaA --> Search[Elasticsearch (indexing)]
  Search --> API
```

### Why this worked
- Strong consistency removed a whole class of "eventual consistency" bugs where reads returned stale data and the app had to reconcile.
- Postgres's predictable tail latency under write-heavy workloads (with tuned WAL and batching) improved user-perceived latency.
- Operational surface shrank: fewer node-level compaction / repair events reduced on-call toil.

### Results (concrete outcomes)
| Metric | Before (Cassandra) | After (Postgres migration) |
|--------|---------------------:|---------------------------:|
| Median read latency | ~20–40 ms | ~10–15 ms |
| 99th-percentile read latency | ~200–300 ms | ~20–40 ms |
| Operator/DBA incident hours per month (approx) | 60–120 hrs | 10–20 hrs |
| Queryability (ad-hoc SQL) | Low (difficult) | High (immediate) |
| Complexity (number of DB technologies in core path) | 3 (Cassandra + caches + search) | 2 (Postgres + search) |

> Real-world effect: tail-latency reductions translated into faster page loads and fewer perceived "stutters" when opening popular posts. On-call time for DB incidents dropped by a large fraction because compaction/repair storms were no longer a regular emergency.

### Tradeoffs & caveats
- Write amplification: Postgres requires more careful WAL and disk IO tuning at scale; Reddit mitigated with careful batching and sharding.
- Operational cost of many Postgres clusters (per-shard primaries) — solved through automation and standardized templates.
- Not a one-size-fits-all: Cassandra stayed for some append-only, very large time-series workloads where its strengths were still valuable.

### Why the migration matters to other engineers
This isn't "SQL vs NoSQL" dogma — it's about fitting the tool to the observable pain. Reddit swapped a distributed eventually-consistent system that required constant firefighting for a strongly-consistent relational system that made software easier to reason about and operate.

### Final lessons (brief story moment)
Migrating a critical datastore is scary. Reddit lowered the blast radius by:
- Building a routing shim to dual-write and dark-launch
- Moving in small keyspace slices and validating with metrics
- Investing in operational automation for Postgres

The result: faster, simpler, and more dependable behavior for communities that depend on instant visibility of their posts and comments.
keyTakeaways: Move incrementally: use a migration adapter and dual-writes/dark-launch to reduce blast radius.
Match data shape to datastore: relational data that needs joins and strong consistency often benefits from Postgres.
Operational work can be a bigger cost than raw throughput — reducing complexity (fewer technologies in the critical path) can pay big dividends (orders-of-magnitude fewer incidents).

concept.title: Cache Smarter, Not Harder: Choosing Between Cache-Aside, Write-Through and Write-Back
concept.summary: Caching can cut latency and reduce load — but the strategy you choose controls correctness, complexity, and throughput. This guide explains cache-aside, write-through, and write-back with analogies, diagrams, code, and before/after comparisons so you can pick the right pattern for your system.
concept.explanation:
## WHY it matters

Reading directly from a database for every request is like walking to the warehouse every time you want a snack: slow and expensive. Caches act like a pantry near the kitchen. But how you keep that pantry stocked and in sync with the warehouse determines whether you get fast responses or stale/incorrect results.

This note explains three common caching strategies — **cache-aside**, **write-through**, and **write-back** — when to use each, practical examples, and common pitfalls.

### The analogies

- Cache-aside: you check the pantry first; if the snack isn't there, you fetch from the warehouse and add it to the pantry. (Lazy loading)
- Write-through: when you restock, you put the item both in the pantry and in the warehouse immediately. (Synchronous write)
- Write-back: you restock the pantry and jot down a note to update the warehouse later; updates are batched. (Asynchronous write)

---

### High-level sequence (cache-aside)

```mermaid
sequenceDiagram
  Client->>App: GET /user/123
  App->>Cache: GET user:123
  Cache-->>App: MISS
  App->>DB: SELECT * FROM users WHERE id=123
  DB-->>App: user data
  App->>Cache: SET user:123
  App-->>Client: 200 OK + user data
```

### How each strategy works (flow summaries)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
  A[Client Read] --> B{Cache-aside}
  B -->|Hit| C[Return Cache]
  B -->|Miss| D[Load DB -> Populate Cache]

  E[Client Write] --> F{Write-through}
  F -->|Write| G[Write Cache]
  G --> H[Write DB]
  H --> I[Ack to Client]

  J[Client Write] --> K{Write-back}
  K -->|Write| L[Write Cache]
  L --> M[Mark Dirty -> Background Flush]
  M --> N[Batch Write DB]
```

---

### Comparison table

| Approach | Pros | Cons | When to use |
|----------|------|------|-------------|
| Cache-aside | Simple, low read latency, no extra work on reads that are misses | More complex reads on cold caches; need careful invalidation on writes | Read-heavy workloads; caches as opportunistic accelerators |
| Write-through | Stronger consistency between cache and DB; reads get recent data | Higher write latency (sync writes to cache and DB); more write load | When reads must see fresh data and writes are moderate |
| Write-back | Low write latency; can batch DB writes (high throughput) | Risk of data loss on cache failure; more complex eviction and durability | High-write systems where occasional staleness is acceptable and durability mitigations exist (battery-backed cache, commit logs) |

---

### BEFORE / AFTER: Adding a cache (example numbers)

| Metric | Before (DB only) | After (cache-aside, 70% hit) |
|--------|------------------|-----------------------------|
| Average read latency | 60 ms | 0.8 * 60ms (miss) + 0.2 * 2ms (hit) ≈ 50 ms → actually improves most reads if hit higher | 
| DB QPS | 1000 r/s | ~300 r/s (70% served by cache) |
| Consistency | Strong | Eventual unless invalidated carefully |

Note: if hit rate increases to 95%, read latency and DB load improve dramatically. The numbers above are illustrative; measure in your environment.

---

### Code examples

Cache-aside (Node.js + Redis pseudo-example)

```js
// get-user.js (cache-aside)
const redis = require('redis');
const db = require('./db'); // your DB client
const cache = redis.createClient();

async function getUser(id) {
  const key = `user:${id}`;
  const cached = await cache.get(key);
  if (cached) return JSON.parse(cached); // cache HIT

  const user = await db.query('SELECT * FROM users WHERE id = $1', [id]); // cache MISS
  if (user) {
    await cache.setEx(key, 60 * 5, JSON.stringify(user)); // TTL 5m
  }
  return user;
}
```

Write-through (pseudo)

```js
// update-user.js (write-through)
async function updateUser(id, patch) {
  const key = `user:${id}`;
  // Write to cache first (or in parallel) then to db synchronously
  await cache.set(key, JSON.stringify(patch));
  const updated = await db.query('UPDATE users SET ... WHERE id = $1 RETURNING *', [id]);
  return updated;
}
```

Write-back (sketch; do NOT use without careful durability)

```js
// write-back sketch
// 1) write to cache
await cache.set(key, JSON.stringify(data));
await cache.hSet('dirty', key, Date.now());
// 2) background worker periodically scans 'dirty' and flushes batches to DB
```

---

### Practical guidance: when to pick which

- Use **cache-aside** if reads are much more frequent than writes, you can tolerate slightly higher latency on a cache miss, and you want simple invalidation semantics.
- Use **write-through** when you need reads to observe fresh data and write latency impact is acceptable. Good for datasets where correctness > absolute write throughput.
- Use **write-back** only when you must optimize write throughput/latency aggressively, and you can add durability safeguards (write-ahead logs, replication of cache, careful flush strategies).

### Common pitfalls and how to avoid them

- Stale reads: failing to invalidate/update cache on writes. Solution: pick an invalidation scheme (delete-on-write, update-on-write) and stick to it.
- Cache stampede: many clients trigger DB load on key expiry. Solution: use locks, randomized TTLs, or request coalescing.
- Eviction surprises: items evicted while marked dirty in write-back -> data loss. Solution: avoid write-back unless cache durability is guaranteed or use write-ahead logging.
- Uneven key distribution: hot keys can overload DB if miss patterns occur. Solution: use TTL tuning, pre-warming, or application-level rate limiting.
- Hidden complexity: write-back introduces lost updates and harder debugging. Prefer simpler patterns unless necessary.

---

### Quick checklist before adding a cache

- Is the workload read-heavy and latency-sensitive? (yes -> cache helps)
- Can you tolerate eventual consistency or stale reads? (cache-aside OK)
- Do writes need to be durable instantly? (prefer write-through or no write-back)
- Do you have metrics and observability to measure hit rates and DB load? (instrument heavily)

### Small operational tips

- Always set sensible TTLs for cache-aside to bound staleness.
- Use versioning or conditional writes for updates (compare-and-set) to avoid races.
- Monitor cache hit ratio, miss origin, eviction rate, and cache latency.

---

### Summary

Caching is not a single tool but a family of trade-offs. Cache-aside is the simplest and most flexible for read-heavy services. Write-through buys fresher reads at the cost of write latency. Write-back can give huge write throughput but adds complexity and risk. Choose based on your consistency, latency, and durability requirements — and measure.
keyTakeaways: Cache-aside: simple and read-optimized; load DB on miss and populate cache.
Write-through: keeps cache and DB synchronized at write time; stronger read freshness, higher write latency.
Write-back: fastest writes via lazy persistence; requires strong durability measures and careful eviction handling.
